### Limitations

- This is a finite classification of deterministic same-anchor `K2-K2`
  two-class witnesses on the recorded fifth-pair frontier only.
- It does not classify different-anchor witnesses, four-class bridge
  witnesses, or the 11 non-two-class cases.
- It does not prove that every possible two-row certificate in a child belongs
  to this family; it classifies the deterministic chosen witness.
- It does not prove an all-order `C19_skew` obstruction, Erdos Problem #97, or
  a counterexample.

### Effect on the Attack

All same-anchor deterministic two-class categories are now parametrized on the
recorded data:

```text
same-anchor K1-K1: 8416 / 8416 covered
same-anchor K1-K2: 7431 / 7431 covered
same-anchor K2-K2:  977 /  977 covered
```

The remaining deterministic two-class categories from Theory Cycle 136 are
all different-anchor categories:

```text
different-anchor K1-K2: 442
different-anchor K1-K1:  91
different-anchor K2-K2:  66
```

The next proof-facing subquestion is whether these different-anchor families
are also small bridge-template families, or whether they require a new
nonlocal quotient pattern.

## Session 2026-05-06, Theory Cycle 142

Timestamp: 2026-05-06 01:48 EEST.

### Subquestion

Do the `442` different-anchor `K1-K2` deterministic two-class witnesses admit
a finite quotient-cancellation template classification?

### Definitions and Assumptions

Use the same selected-distance quotient and deterministic two-class filter as
Theory Cycles 136-141. For unordered pairs, write `[uv]` for the quotient
class. The rows are:

```text
K1(h,a,b,c): [hb] + [ac] > [ha] + [bc]
K2(g,p,q,r): [gq] + [pr] > [gr] + [pq]
```

In this cycle the `K1` and `K2` anchors are different. If the deterministic
prefilter returned the rows in `K2-K1` order, the replay normalizes the row
order to list the `K1` row first before classifying the pair.

### Attempted Proof Route

First isolate the different-anchor mixed category. The exact replay gives:

```text
recorded fifth-pair children checked: 18270
deterministic two-class witnesses: 17423
deterministic four-class witnesses: 836
deterministic two-row misses or one-row cases: 11
different-anchor K1-K2 two-class witnesses: 442
normalized different-anchor K1-K2 digest:
  a45de75e1275bafd0fca9c45152f30826d96f004454ecf2d5a86342e45b7b73d
```

The leading literal row pairs are:

```text
199  K1(0,3,8,17)   + K2(1,3,17,12)
 56  K1(1,15,4,5)   + K2(0,4,9,5)
 25  K1(0,14,8,6)   + K2(1,8,17,6)
 15  K1(1,15,11,5)  + K2(0,11,16,5)
 13  K1(0,3,10,2)   + K2(3,5,14,2)
```

Expanding the top pairs suggests four dominant bridge templates. After those
cover `400` witnesses, the remaining `42` split into low-count variants.

### Result

Finite classification:
**Recorded Different-Anchor K1-K2 Two-Class Classification**.

The dominant four templates are:

```text
shared-ac:
  K1(h,a,b,c) + K2(g,a,c,d)
  [hb]=[bc], [gc]=[gd], [ad]=[ha]

shared-bc-outer:
  K1(h,a,b,c) + K2(g,b,d,c)
  [ac]=[ha], [gd]=[gc], [bd]=[hb]

anchor-a-tail-c:
  K1(h,a,b,c) + K2(a,d,e,c)
  [hb]=[bc], [dc]=[de], [ae]=[ha]

shared-bc-inner:
  K1(h,a,b,c) + K2(g,b,d,c)
  [hb]=[ha], [gd]=[bd], [gc]=[ac]
```

In each case the equalities reduce the two strict rows to opposite
inequalities. For example, `shared-ac` reduces the `K1` row to
`[ac] > [ha]` and the `K2` row to `[ha] > [ac]`.

The late templates needed for the `42`-witness residue are:

```text
shared-ac-alt:
  K1(h,a,b,c) + K2(g,a,c,d)
  [hb]=[ha], [ad]=[gd], [gc]=[bc]

third-a-fourth-c:
  K1(h,a,b,c) + K2(g,d,a,c)
  [hb]=[bc], [dc]=[da], [ga]=[ha], [gc]=[ac]

anchor-a-tail-c-alt:
  K1(h,a,b,c) + K2(a,d,e,c)
  [hb]=[ha], [ae]=[de], [dc]=[bc]

anchor-b-third-c:
  K1(h,a,b,c) + K2(b,d,c,e)
  [ac]=[ha], [de]=[dc], [be]=[hb]

second-a-fourth-b:
  K1(h,a,b,c) + K2(g,a,d,b)
  [hb]=[ha], [gd]=[gb], [ab]=[bc], [ad]=[ac]

anchor-a-third-b:
  K1(h,a,b,c) + K2(a,d,b,e)
  [ac]=[bc], [de]=[ae], [ab]=[ha], [db]=[hb]

g-d-h-a:
  K1(h,a,b,c) + K2(g,d,h,a)
  [ac]=[bc], [gh]=[ga], [da]=[ha], [dh]=[hb]

g-d-b-c-inner:
  K1(h,a,b,c) + K2(g,d,b,c)
  [hb]=[ha], [dc]=[db], [gb]=[bc], [gc]=[ac]

g-d-b-c-outer:
  K1(h,a,b,c) + K2(g,d,b,c)
  [ac]=[ha], [gb]=[gc], [dc]=[bc], [db]=[hb]
```

The final exact replay gives:

```text
recorded fifth-pair children checked: 18270
deterministic two-class witnesses: 17423
deterministic four-class witnesses: 836
deterministic two-row misses or one-row cases: 11
different-anchor K1-K2 two-class witnesses: 442
covered by ordered different-anchor K1-K2 template family: 442
different-anchor K1-K2 residual after ordered family: 0
```

The ordered partition counts are:

```text

anchor-a-tail-c:        45
shared-bc-inner:        29
```

The all-hit counts agree with the ordered counts, so no overlaps were observed.

The template digests are:

### Limitations

- This is a finite classification of deterministic different-anchor `K1-K2`
  two-class witnesses on the recorded fifth-pair frontier only.
- It does not classify different-anchor `K1-K1` or `K2-K2` witnesses.
- It does not classify four-class bridge witnesses or the 11 non-two-class
  cases beyond earlier recorded results.
- It does not prove that every possible two-row certificate in a child belongs
  to this family; it classifies the deterministic chosen witness.
- It does not prove an all-order `C19_skew` obstruction, Erdos Problem #97, or
  a counterexample.

### Effect on the Attack

The largest different-anchor deterministic two-class category is now
parametrized on the recorded data. Together with the same-anchor
classifications, the covered deterministic two-class categories are:

```text
same-anchor K1-K1:        8416 / 8416
same-anchor K1-K2:        7431 / 7431
same-anchor K2-K2:         977 /  977
different-anchor K1-K2:    442 /  442
```

The remaining deterministic two-class categories from Theory Cycle 136 are:

```text
different-anchor K1-K1: 91
different-anchor K2-K2: 66
```

The next proof-facing subquestion is whether these two final small categories
close by variants of the same different-anchor bridge templates.

  `codex/goal-erdos97-proof-or-counterexample`.

- The worktree was already dirty before this cycle; this cycle changes only

### Chosen Research Direction

Checkpoint direction: exact Kalmanson certificate diagnostics supporting open
issues #81 and #83.

Rationale:

- The all-order C13/C19 problem is too large for an unfocused first step.
- The repo already has exact fixed-order Kalmanson certificates but only a
  C19-specific static diagnostic artifact.
- A deterministic analyzer that validates certificates and summarizes support
  structure, gap patterns, distance-class cancellations, and rank data gives a
  reproducible base for later C13 cyclic-order search and C19 decomposition.
- This is a checkpoint artifact only. It does not change the global status and
  does not claim an abstract all-order obstruction.

1. Enforced branch safety and moved off `main`.
2. Completed orientation over state, claim, failed-route, round-two, script,
   source, test, and GitHub issue context.
3. Established the test baseline in the existing `.venv`.
4. Selected exact Kalmanson certificate diagnostics as the focused checkpoint.
5. Added `scripts/analyze_kalmanson_certificates.py`, which rechecks the
   registered fixed-order C13 and C19 Kalmanson/Farkas certificates and emits
   deterministic support diagnostics.
6. Generated `reports/kalmanson_certificate_diagnostics.json`.
7. Added `tests/test_kalmanson_certificate_diagnostics.py`.
8. Added `docs/kalmanson-certificate-diagnostics.md` and linked it from

### Focus

Bounded C13 Kalmanson cyclic-order pilot.

certificate diagnostics checkpoint. The goal is not to prove the all-order C13
case, but to make the first small fixed-order pilot reproducible and
independently checkable.

The pilot normalizes seven pinned cyclic orders by rotation/reflection and
runs the fixed-order Kalmanson/Farkas certificate finder on each unique order.
All seven unique orders close by exact positive-integer certificates. The
committed artifact stores compact checked summaries; the script can emit the
full certificate objects by omitting `--summary-only`.

## Session 2026-05-03, Iteration 3

### Branch and Local State

- Current branch: `codex/goal-erdos97-proof-or-counterexample`.

  present and is still treated as out of scope.

### Focus

Bounded C13 Kalmanson prefix brancher.

This follows open issue #81 more closely than the pinned-order pilot by adding
reflection-prefix pruning before LP/certificate calls. It is still a bounded
pilot, not an all-order C13 search.

### Work Performed

- Added `scripts/branch_c13_kalmanson_prefix_pilot.py`.
- Added `data/certificates/c13_kalmanson_prefix_branch_pilot.json`.
- Added `tests/test_c13_kalmanson_prefix_branch_pilot.py`.
- Added `docs/c13-kalmanson-prefix-branch-pilot.md`.
- Linked the brancher from `docs/index.md`, `docs/c13-kalmanson-order-pilot.md`,
  and `docs/kalmanson-c13-pilot.md`.

The brancher fixes label `0`, branches on two labels from each side of the
cyclic order, prunes reflection duplicates as soon as the boundary prefix is
decided, and then runs a deterministic budget of twelve fixed-order
Kalmanson/Farkas certificate searches on ascending completions. The default
run has 11,880 raw boundary states, 5,940 canonical states after reflection
pruning, 66 prefix extensions pruned before LP, and 12/12 sampled completions
closed by exact positive-integer certificates.

### Verification Commands

- `.venv/bin/python scripts/branch_c13_kalmanson_prefix_pilot.py --assert-expected --out data/certificates/c13_kalmanson_prefix_branch_pilot.json`:
  passed.
- `.venv/bin/python scripts/branch_c13_kalmanson_prefix_pilot.py --assert-expected --include-certificates --out /tmp/c13_prefix_branch_full_certificates.json`:
  passed.
- `.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_c13_kalmanson_prefix_branch_pilot.py tests/test_c13_kalmanson_order_pilot.py tests/test_kalmanson_certificate.py`:
  passed, `8 passed`.
- `.venv/bin/python scripts/check_text_clean.py`: passed.
- `.venv/bin/python scripts/check_status_consistency.py`: passed.
- `git diff --check`: passed.
- `.venv/bin/python -m pytest -q`: passed, `175 passed, 8 deselected`.

### Claim Boundary

- Trust label: `EXACT_CERTIFICATE_DIAGNOSTIC`.
- Reflection pruning is search accounting, not a geometric obstruction.
- Exact obstructions apply only to the twelve sampled fixed cyclic orders in
  the artifact.
- No general proof, no counterexample, and no all-order C13 obstruction are
  claimed.

### Focus

Partial-branch C13 Kalmanson closures.

This strengthens the previous prefix-branch pilot by replacing sampled full
cyclic-order completions with certificates that use only Kalmanson
inequalities forced by a two-sided boundary prefix. A closed prefix branch is
therefore killed for every completion of that prefix.

### Work Performed

- Added `scripts/certify_c13_kalmanson_partial_branches.py`.
- Added `data/certificates/c13_kalmanson_partial_branch_closures.json`.
- Added `tests/test_c13_kalmanson_partial_branches.py`.

  `docs/c13-kalmanson-prefix-branch-pilot.md`,
  `docs/c13-kalmanson-order-pilot.md`, and `docs/kalmanson-c13-pilot.md`.

The scan uses the 5,940 canonical two-boundary-pair states. For each branch,
it keeps only the 170 Kalmanson inequalities whose quadrilateral order is
forced by the prefix. Exact positive-integer Farkas certificates close 5,108
branches; 832 two-boundary-pair prefixes remain unclosed by this pass. The
compact artifact records closure counts, support-size histograms, twelve
checked certificate examples, and a digest plus first/last windows for the
832 unclosed branch labels.

### Focus

C13 Kalmanson third-pair refinement.

This follows the 832-prefix frontier left by the two-boundary-pair pass. The
goal is to replace a large unresolved frontier with a smaller exact frontier by
adding one more forced left/right boundary pair and using only Kalmanson
inequalities forced by that deeper prefix.

### Work Performed

- Added `scripts/refine_c13_kalmanson_third_pair.py`.
- Added `data/certificates/c13_kalmanson_third_pair_refinement.json`.
- Added `tests/test_c13_kalmanson_third_pair_refinement.py`.

  `docs/c13-kalmanson-partial-branch-closures.md`,
  `docs/c13-kalmanson-order-pilot.md`, and `docs/kalmanson-c13-pilot.md`.

The scan recomputes the 832 unclosed canonical two-boundary-pair parents,
adds one ordered left/right boundary pair to each parent, and checks each
third-pair child using only prefix-forced Kalmanson rows. Each parent has 56
children. The full pass scans 46,592 child branches, closes 46,567 by exact
positive-integer Farkas certificates, and leaves 25 child branches unclosed by
this pass.

## Session 2026-05-06, Theory Cycle 143

Timestamp: 2026-05-06 02:04 EEST.

### Subquestion

Do the remaining `91` recorded different-anchor `K1-K1` deterministic
two-class witnesses admit a finite quotient-cancellation template
classification?

### Definitions and Assumptions

Use the selected-distance quotient from the recorded C19 fifth-pair frontier.
For a forced Kalmanson row

```text
K1(a,b,c,d): [ac] + [bd] > [ab] + [cd],
```

write `ac=ab`, for example, to mean equality of the two quotient classes
`[ac]` and `[ab]`. Such an equality cancels one positive term and one negative
term inside the row. A deterministic two-class `K1-K1` witness is one in which
each K1 row reduces to exactly one strict comparison between two quotient
classes, and the second row supplies the opposite strict comparison.

The replay keeps the `25` different-anchor `K1-K1` four-class bridge witnesses
separate; they are not part of this two-class subquestion.

### Attempted Proof Route

Run the deterministic two-row lookup on the combined recorded fifth-pair
frontier, then filter to row pairs in which both rows are `K1` rows, the row
anchors differ, and both rows have exactly two nonzero quotient classes after
internal cancellation. Classify each target by:

- the internal cancellation in the first K1 row;
- the internal cancellation in the second K1 row; and
- the two cross equalities identifying the residual comparison in one row with
  the opposite residual comparison in the other.

### Result

Proved finite-template lemma:
**Different-Anchor K1-K1 Two-Class Quotient Cancellation Lemma**.

Let

```text
A = K1(a,b,c,d)
B = K1(e,f,g,h).
```

In the following table, `A.bd=B.ab` means the quotient classes represented by
the `bd` term of row `A` and the `ab` term of row `B` agree. Each listed
signature reduces row `A` to one strict inequality `X > Y` and row `B` to the
opposite strict inequality `Y > X`.

```text
name              A cancellation  A residual  B cancellation  B residual  cross equalities                   count
D1_acab_acdc      ac=ab           bd>cd       ac=cd           bd>ab       A.bd=B.ab, A.cd=B.bd              21
D2_accd_bdab      ac=cd           bd>ab       bd=ab           ac>cd       A.bd=B.cd, A.ab=B.ac              19
D3_bdab_bdcd      bd=ab           ac>cd       bd=cd           ac>ab       A.ac=B.ab, A.cd=B.ac              18
D4_bdab_accd      bd=ab           ac>cd       ac=cd           bd>ab       A.ac=B.ab, A.cd=B.bd              15
D5_acab_acab      ac=ab           bd>cd       ac=ab           bd>cd       A.bd=B.cd, A.cd=B.bd              11
D6_accd_bdcd      ac=cd           bd>ab       bd=cd           ac>ab       A.bd=B.ab, A.ab=B.ac               5
D7_accd_acab      ac=cd           bd>ab       ac=ab           bd>cd       A.bd=B.cd, A.ab=B.bd               1
D8_bdab_acab      bd=ab           ac>cd       ac=ab           bd>cd       A.ac=B.cd, A.cd=B.bd               1
```

The proof is direct in every row of the table. For instance,
`D1_acab_acdc` reduces row `A` to `A.bd > A.cd` and row `B` to
`B.bd > B.ab`. The cross equalities `A.bd=B.ab` and `A.cd=B.bd` turn these into
`B.ab > B.bd` and `B.bd > B.ab`, a contradiction. The other seven signatures
are the same two-step cancellation with different positional terms.

The exact replay over the recorded target gives:

```text
different-anchor K1-K1 two-class witnesses: 91
different-anchor K1-K1 four-class bridge witnesses excluded: 25
target digest:
  de9497cf2ff1c3d40a7a37bbaa50245817946d192296c842fef047531459cce0
ordered assignment digest:
  b20760a737ee180946a26987e57bdc1ff9d999e2d62a18cf2d6d16c4df8b0063
```

The leading literal row-pair hits are:

```text
20  D1_acab_acdc  K1(1,10,17,12) + K1(10,12,5,2)
16  D3_bdab_bdcd  K1(0,10,8,7)   + K1(8,13,7,2)
14  D4_bdab_accd  K1(8,16,18,2)  + K1(15,18,10,2)
12  D2_accd_bdab  K1(10,18,5,2)  + K1(8,16,18,2)
 5  D5_acab_acab  K1(4,13,15,2)  + K1(10,15,7,2)
```

### Limitations

- This is a finite classification of deterministic different-anchor `K1-K1`
  two-class witnesses on the recorded fifth-pair frontier only.
- It excludes the `25` different-anchor `K1-K1` four-class bridge witnesses,
  which belong to the earlier bridge-template analysis rather than this
  two-class cancellation lemma.
- It classifies the deterministic chosen two-row witness, not every possible
  two-row certificate in the same child.
- It does not classify different-anchor `K2-K2` witnesses.
- It does not prove an all-order `C19_skew` obstruction, Erdos Problem #97, or
  a counterexample.

### Effect on the Attack

The deterministic two-class comparison side is now classified through all
categories except different-anchor `K2-K2`:

```text
same-anchor K1-K1:        8416 / 8416
same-anchor K1-K2:        7431 / 7431
same-anchor K2-K2:         977 /  977
different-anchor K1-K2:    442 /  442
different-anchor K1-K1:     91 /   91
```

The remaining deterministic two-class category from Theory Cycle 136 is:

```text
different-anchor K2-K2: 66
```

The next proof-facing subquestion is whether the `66` different-anchor `K2-K2`
witnesses admit the analogous quotient-cancellation classification.

## 2026-05-07 - Cycle 557 - Full Shared-Endpoint RX Transport Audit

### Subquestion

Cycles 555 and 556 tested six frequent mixed one-term off-target families of
the form

```text
Ra < Xb_a
```

where the radius label `a` is one endpoint of the pair class. The narrow
question for this cycle was:

```text
In the sampled C19 prefix-window artifact, does the shared-endpoint shift a
work for every zero target-aware forbidden placement carrying any off-target
one-term RX family Ra < Xb_a?
```

Equivalently, is there any sampled source placement where shifting the center
by the radius endpoint fails to expose the Cycle 549 target-aware 22-family
vocabulary?

### Definitions and Setup

The audit uses the finite catalog-prefilter artifact

```text
data/certificates/c19_kalmanson_prefix_window_catalog_prefilter_sweep_288_479.json
```

and reconstructs all recorded fifth-pair children. For a child and center
`c`, set `p=c+7 mod 19`. The forbidden depth classes are the six classes from
Cycles 549-556:

```text
(LD, LD), (LD, RD), (M, LD), (RD, LD), (RD, M), (RD, RD)
```

For each child, the audit enumerates all exact inverse pairs `v,-v` among
forced Kalmanson row quotient vectors. A placement `(child,c,p)` is zero
target-aware if no inverse-pair vector touches either the selected-radius class
`R_c` or the pair class `X_{c,p}`. Families are normalized by translating
`c` to `0`.

A one-term RX family is included in this cycle exactly when it has the form
`Ra < Xb_a` after normalization. Its shared-endpoint shift is the center
shift by `a`; the other endpoint shift is by `b`.

### Result Status

Promising finite reduction:
**Full Shared-Endpoint RX Transport Audit**.

In the sampled windows 288-479, every source placement carrying any one-term
RX family `Ra < Xb_a` transports under the shared-endpoint shift `a` into the
Cycle 549 target-aware 22-family vocabulary.

This is still a finite sampled statement, not a proof of the conjecture
outside the recorded artifact.

### Exact Audit Result

The audit reproduced the previous sampled totals:

```text
fifth-pair children: 10350
forbidden placements: 70614
zero target-aware placements: 27255
target-aware vocabulary size: 22
target family-count histogram:
  0: 27255
  1: 18313
  2: 11915
  3: 7017
  4: 3634
  5: 1397
  6: 708
  7: 247
  8: 96
  9: 26
  10: 6
```

The full shared-RX transport counts were:

```text
shared RX families tested: 161
shared RX source occurrences: 209185
families with shared-shift failures: 0
shared-shift failure occurrences: 0
families with other-endpoint failures: 144
other-endpoint failure occurrences: 73545
families with no forbidden target-aware shift: 120
no-forbidden-target-aware-shift occurrences: 599
```

After removing the six families already tested in Cycles 555-556, this cycle
adds:

```text
new shared RX families tested: 155
new shared RX source occurrences: 190808
new shared-shift failure occurrences: 0
```

The audit digest was:

```text
a7dda13294135b13dc7a4367f8e56c62c3c9b62aebfbb5582898351ff2ca87b1
```

### Top New Families

The most frequent newly tested shared-RX families were:

```text
family          sources  children  shared_shift  shared_success  other_shift  other_success
R18 < X6_18       2798      2589            18            2798            6           1704
R4 < X4_17        2772      2540             4            2772           17           1822
R15 < X3_15       2619      2415            15            2619            3           1439
R9 < X7_9         2585      2323             9            2585            7           1846
R12 < X6_12       2583      2369            12            2583            6           1702
R16 < X10_16      2521      2348            16            2521           10           1875
R16 < X4_16       2460      2274            16            2460            4           1774
R15 < X14_15      2439      2211            15            2439           14           1397
R11 < X11_18      2402      2234            11            2402           18           1597
R1 < X1_18        2382      2185             1            2382           18           1750
```

Every listed shared success count equals the source count. The same equality
holds for all 161 tested families.

### Obstruction to the Other-Endpoint Rule

The other endpoint remains unreliable. For the most frequent newly tested
family, `R18 < X6_18`, the first source where the other shift `6` fails but
the shared shift `18` succeeds is:

```text
label c19_window_fifth_child_0338_0063_0006
source center 15, partner 3, depth (RD, LD)
boundary_left [1, 3, 17, 11, 6]
boundary_right [2, 5, 4, 15, 14]
remaining [7, 8, 9, 10, 12, 13, 16, 18]
shared_shift 18
other_shift 6
good_offsets [2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 10, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 18]
forbidden_good_offsets [2, 3, 8, 14]
```

Thus the broader endpoint rule is false well beyond the six high-frequency
families from Cycles 555-556:

```text
For Ra < Xb_a, both endpoint shifts a and b expose the target-aware
vocabulary.
```

### Obstruction to the Stronger Forbidden-Shift Route

The stronger route requiring some shifted target-aware placement to remain in
a forbidden depth class also fails broadly. For `R18 < X6_18`, the first
source with target-aware shifts but no forbidden target-aware shift is:

```text
label c19_window_fifth_child_0368_0025_0037
source center 3, partner 10, depth (LD, RD)
boundary_left [1, 3, 4, 8, 13]
boundary_right [2, 6, 10, 11, 7]
remaining [5, 9, 12, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18]
shared_shift 18
other_shift 6
good_offsets [2, 5, 16, 18]
forbidden_good_offsets []
```

So the surviving route is still target-vocabulary transport, not
forbidden-class-preserving transport.

### Argument

The audit is an exact finite replay over the stored sampled artifact. It uses
the existing prefix-forced Kalmanson row construction and selected-distance
quotient map, then enumerates inverse pairs by exact integer quotient vectors.
For each zero target-aware forbidden placement, it filters the complete
off-target family set to one-term RX families whose radius label is a pair
endpoint. For each such family `Ra < Xb_a`, it checks the shifted placement
with center `c+a`.

The shared shift succeeds in every one of the 209185 source occurrences. Since
the audit found no shared-shift failure, there is no sampled obstruction to
the shared-endpoint conjecture among one-term RX families in windows 288-479.
The displayed other-endpoint and forbidden-shift witnesses show that two
stronger natural variants are false.

### Limitations

- This is a finite sampled audit over windows 288-479 only.
- It covers one-term RX off-target families in the sampled artifact, not all
  off-target family types from Cycle 551.
- It remains a replay computation, not a human-readable cyclic-order proof.
- The shared-endpoint RX transport principle remains unproved outside the
  sampled artifact.
- The result does not prove or refute an all-order `C19_skew` obstruction.
- It does not prove a general theorem for Erdos Problem #97 and does not give
  a counterexample.

### Effect on the Attack

Cycles 555-556 suggested the shared endpoint as the correct transport choice
for mixed radius/pair families. This cycle upgrades that sampled evidence from
six hand-picked frequent families to every one-term RX family of the form
`Ra < Xb_a` appearing in the sampled zero target-aware source set.

The theory target is now sharper: prove a local order/quotient lemma showing
why the radius endpoint `a` of `Ra < Xb_a` must make some target-aware inverse
pair visible after shifting the center by `a`. Any proof route that treats the
other endpoint symmetrically is ruled out by exact sampled witnesses.

### Next Lead

Try to prove the shared-endpoint RX transport principle directly from the
cyclic order and selected-distance quotient. A useful first lemma would state
precise hypotheses on a forced inverse pair of type `Ra < Xb_a` and derive
the existence of a target-aware inverse pair after translating the center by
`a`, without requiring the shifted placement to remain forbidden.

If that proof stalls, repeat the same exhaustive sampled transport audit for
the remaining non-RX off-target family types from Cycle 551, keeping the
family-type statements separate.

## Session 2026-05-06, Theory Cycle 412

Timestamp: 2026-05-06 15:18 EEST.

### Subquestion

Does the forest property from Theory Cycle 411 reflect a stronger finite
recorded-frontier implication: whenever the mixed bidirectional residual graph
`G_mix` has a cycle, must the same state also have a selected-selected
bidirectional residual trigger?

### Definitions and Assumptions

Use the same C19 quotient, singly reduced rows, residual directed edges, and
mixed bidirectional residual graph `G_mix` as in Theory Cycle 411. A state has
an **R-R trigger** if it contains a two-row quotient-cancellation pair whose
opposite residual classes are both selected classes `R_i`.

The bucket names are:

```text
both:       at least one R-R trigger and at least one mixed trigger
mixed only: at least one mixed trigger and no R-R trigger
R-R only:   at least one R-R trigger and no mixed trigger
none:       no two-row meta-lemma trigger
```

### Attempted Proof Route

Replay the same `18,270` recorded fifth-pair states from windows `128-287` and
`288-479`. For each state, build `G_mix`, compute its cyclomatic number and
girth, and compare this with the trigger bucket. If a cycle in `G_mix` always
implies an R-R trigger, then the mixed-only forest lemma can be restated as a
finite contrapositive.

### Result

Proved finite recorded-frontier implication:
**Mixed Cycle Forces R-R Trigger in the Recorded Frontier**.

In the recorded fifth-pair frontier, every state whose `G_mix` has a cycle is
in the `both` bucket. There are no states with a cyclic mixed graph and no
R-R trigger.

The exact replay re-derived the bucket split:

```text
both:       17687
mixed only:   536
R-R only:      36
none:          11
```

The source split is:

```text
128-287 both:       7618
128-287 mixed only:  277
128-287 R-R only:     22
128-287 none:          3
288-479 both:      10069
288-479 mixed only:  259
288-479 R-R only:     14
288-479 none:          8
```

The cyclomatic histogram by bucket is:

```text
both:       0:17681, 1:6
mixed only: 0:536
R-R only:   0:36
none:       0:11
```

The girth histogram by bucket is:

```text
both:       0:17681, 6:2, 8:4
mixed only: 0:536
R-R only:   0:36
none:       0:11
```

Thus the finite implication

```text
G_mix cyclic  =>  state has an R-R trigger
```

has zero recorded violations. The mixed-cycle digest is:

```text
f5768a7c5c2ac79d34541614102e15b8904c44a503012c0c9ed8213774cc2cb6
```

The implication is weak in the other direction. Most `both` states still have
forest-shaped mixed graphs:

```text
both states with forest-shaped G_mix: 17681
both states with cyclic G_mix:            6
```

The six cyclic examples are:

```text
c19_window_fifth_child_0168_0075_0052: e=14, cyclomatic=1, girth=6
c19_window_fifth_child_0406_0044_0016: e=21, cyclomatic=1, girth=8
c19_window_fifth_child_0433_0044_0016: e=14, cyclomatic=1, girth=8
c19_window_fifth_child_0436_0130_0025: e=16, cyclomatic=1, girth=6
c19_window_fifth_child_0446_0033_0016: e=19, cyclomatic=1, girth=8
c19_window_fifth_child_0446_0044_0016: e=14, cyclomatic=1, girth=8
```

### Limitations

- This is a finite implication over the recorded `18,270` fifth-pair states
  only.
- It does not prove the local C19 quotient theorem that any possible mixed
  graph cycle forces an R-R trigger.
- It does not explain the `17,681` `both` states whose mixed graph is still a
  forest.
- It does not address the `11` non-meta states or larger supports.
- It does not prove Erdos Problem #97 or produce a counterexample.

### Effect on the Attack

The cycle route explains the forest property of mixed-only states in the
recorded frontier, but only as a finite contrapositive. It is too weak to be a
complete structural explanation of the `both` bucket, because almost every
`both` state also has a forest-shaped `G_mix`.

The next proof-facing lead is to inspect the six cyclic examples and identify
the exact R-R trigger forced there. If the R-R edge can be derived directly
from a mixed cycle, this may become a small local quotient lemma. If not, the
forest observation should be treated as finite structure rather than a
promising finite-to-infinite bridge.

## Session 2026-05-06, Theory Cycle 413

Timestamp: 2026-05-06 15:21 EEST.

### Subquestion

For the six cyclic `G_mix` examples isolated in Theory Cycle 412, is the
accompanying R-R trigger forced directly inside the mixed cycle, or at least
inside the same mixed connected component?

### Definitions and Assumptions

Use the same residual graph definitions as Theory Cycles 411 and 412. For a
state with cyclic `G_mix`, define:

- the **cycle core** as the vertex set remaining after repeatedly deleting
  leaves from `G_mix`;
- an **internal cycle R-R trigger** as an R-R trigger whose two selected
  endpoints both lie in the cycle core; and
- a **same-component R-R trigger** as an R-R trigger whose two selected
  endpoints both lie in the mixed connected component containing the cycle.

If either condition held uniformly, the finite implication from Cycle 412
would have a plausible local proof route: the mixed cycle would visibly
generate an R-R contradiction nearby.

### Attempted Proof Route

Reconstruct exactly the six cyclic states from Cycle 412. For each state,
enumerate all forced singly reduced rows, build `G_mix`, recover the unique
cycle core, enumerate all selected-selected bidirectional residual triggers,
and test whether any such R-R trigger is internal to the core or internal to
the mixed connected component.

### Result

Counterexample to a stronger local-cycle subclaim:
**Mixed Cycles Do Not Carry Their R-R Trigger Locally**.

For all six recorded cyclic examples:

```text
internal cycle R-R triggers:   0
same-component R-R triggers:   0
R-R triggers touching component: positive
```

Thus the observed implication

```text
G_mix cyclic  =>  state has an R-R trigger
```

is not explained by an R-R edge between selected vertices on the mixed cycle,
nor even by an R-R edge wholly inside the mixed component containing the
cycle. The R-R trigger exists elsewhere in the state, although some R-R edges
touch the cycle component at one selected endpoint.

The exact six-state extraction digest is:

```text
2e56cd9f7d49fef1a4f14358785dab7badef9a0bcb2e3dea4e22be90db0ccd4a
```

The cycle-core summaries are:

```text
c19_window_fifth_child_0168_0075_0052:
  core selected vertices: R02, R10, R14
  R-R total=9, internal=0, same-component=0, touching=4

c19_window_fifth_child_0406_0044_0016:
  core selected vertices: R06, R07, R09, R17
  R-R total=5, internal=0, same-component=0, touching=2

c19_window_fifth_child_0433_0044_0016:
  core selected vertices: R06, R07, R09, R17
  R-R total=5, internal=0, same-component=0, touching=2

c19_window_fifth_child_0436_0130_0025:
  core selected vertices: R03, R07, R15
  R-R total=4, internal=0, same-component=0, touching=2

c19_window_fifth_child_0446_0033_0016:
  core selected vertices: R06, R07, R09, R17
  R-R total=5, internal=0, same-component=0, touching=2

c19_window_fifth_child_0446_0044_0016:
  core selected vertices: R06, R07, R09, R17
  R-R total=5, internal=0, same-component=0, touching=2
```

A representative six-cycle is:

```text
R14 -- X4@10 -- R10 -- X6@15 -- R02 -- X7@14 -- R14
```

and a representative eight-cycle is:

```text
R17 -- X6@11 -- R06 -- X1@06 -- R07 -- X2@07 -- R09 -- X6@03 -- R17
```

Neither carries an R-R trigger among its selected cycle vertices.

### Limitations

- This is a finite obstruction to a stronger proof route, using only the six
  recorded cyclic examples.
- It does not refute the weaker finite implication from Theory Cycle 412.
- It does not prove that a more global state-level argument cannot derive an
  R-R trigger from a mixed cycle plus additional boundary data.
- It does not address the `536` mixed-only forest states, the `11` non-meta
  states, or larger supports.
- It does not prove Erdos Problem #97 or produce a counterexample.

### Effect on the Attack

The local mixed-cycle route is weaker than hoped. A direct graph theorem of
the form "a mixed cycle contains an R-R chord" is false on exact recorded
examples. Any proof of the Cycle 412 implication would need to use additional
state data outside the mixed component, not just the cycle graph.

This pushes the proof search back toward either:

1. a leaf-induction description of mixed-only forests; or
2. higher-support certificates for the remaining non-meta and hard mixed
   cases.

The next exact lead is to test whether leaves of `G_mix` have a uniform row
template or boundary placement in the `536` mixed-only states.

## Session 2026-05-06, Theory Cycle 414

Timestamp: 2026-05-06 15:27 EEST.

### Subquestion

Do the leaves of the mixed bidirectional residual graph `G_mix` in the `536`
mixed-only forest states have a uniform residual family, row-template, or
boundary-placement description?

### Definitions and Assumptions

Use the same C19 quotient and `G_mix` definition as Theory Cycles 411--413. A
**leaf edge** is an edge of `G_mix` incident to at least one degree-one vertex.
For each leaf edge, record:

- its translation-normalized mixed residual family, such as `R00--X7@00`;
- whether the selected endpoint is a leaf, the singleton endpoint is a leaf,
  or both endpoints are leaves;
- the unordered pair of single-row cancellation types that witness the two
  opposite residual directions; and
- a coarse boundary side key for the selected center and the singleton chord
  endpoints.

If the mixed-only forest route were close to a proof, one might expect every
mixed-only state to have a leaf in a small family, or a small row-template
catalog, or a small boundary-side pattern.

### Attempted Proof Route

Replay the `18,270` recorded fifth-pair states, recover the same `536`
mixed-only states, build `G_mix` for each, and classify all leaf edges. Then
test exact set covers by leaf residual family and greedy covers by row-template
or boundary-side keys.

### Result

Failed compression:
**Mixed-Only Leaf Catalog Is Still Large**.

The replay re-derived the bucket split:

```text
both:       17687
mixed only:   536
R-R only:      36
none:          11
```

The exact leaf incidence digest is:

```text
79ef6d300b9cff31128f4981a15b3fb0e185e69dd11daf3460db620e4c4510ff
```

The number of leaf edges per mixed-only state is broad:

```text
1:34, 2:38, 3:56, 4:78, 5:63, 6:68, 7:70, 8:39,
9:28, 10:23, 11:17, 12:4, 13:9, 14:5, 15:4
```

Leaf endpoint types across all leaf-edge occurrences are:

```text
both endpoints leaves: 1887
singleton endpoint leaf only: 1122
selected endpoint leaf only: 70
```

The leaf residual-family state counts are:

```text
R00--X7@00: 359
R00--X6@13: 338
R00--X1@00: 270
R00--X2@17: 241
R00--X7@12: 220
R00--X6@05: 212
R00--X4@00: 167
R00--X2@00: 131
R00--X4@15: 130
R00--X1@18: 123
```

An exact minimum cover by leaf residual families still needs `9` of the `10`
families:

```text
R00--X1@00
R00--X1@18
R00--X2@00
R00--X2@17
R00--X4@15
R00--X6@05
R00--X6@13
R00--X7@00
R00--X7@12
```

The omitted family in this minimum cover is `R00--X4@00`. Thus leaf selection
does not improve the residual-family cover size from Theory Cycle 154.

Adding row-template data fragments the leaf catalog further:

```text
leaf row-template signatures: 323
greedy leaf row-template cover size: 42
```

The leading leaf row-template signatures cover only modest portions of the
`536` mixed-only states:

```text
77 R00--X6@13, both endpoints leaves,
   K2 ac=bc leaves bd>ad || K2 ac=bc leaves bd>ad

73 R00--X7@00, both endpoints leaves,
   K1 bd=ab leaves ac>cd || K2 ac=bc leaves bd>ad

72 R00--X1@00, both endpoints leaves,
   K2 ac=bc leaves bd>ad || K2 bd=ad leaves ac>bc

63 R00--X1@00, both endpoints leaves,
   K2 ac=bc leaves bd>ad || K2 ac=bc leaves bd>ad
```

Boundary-side data is still less compressive:

```text
leaf boundary-side keys: 681
greedy leaf boundary-side cover size: 79
```

The leading side key covers only `52` mixed-only states:

```text
R00--X7@00, both endpoints leaves, Rside=L5, Xside=A0+L5
```

### Limitations

- This is a finite recorded-frontier census over the `536` mixed-only states.
- The row-template and side-key cover sizes are diagnostics; only the
  residual-family cover size was minimized exactly by brute force over ten
  families.
- The result does not rule out a subtler leaf invariant using more algebraic
  structure than the recorded keys.
- It does not address the `11` non-meta states or larger supports.
- It does not prove Erdos Problem #97 or produce a counterexample.

### Effect on the Attack

The leaf-induction route does not currently yield a small human-scale proof
object. Leaves exist in every mixed-only forest state, but the leaf families,
row templates, and boundary-side placements remain broad. This makes a direct
leaf-template proof unattractive.

The next proof-facing lead is to stop trying to compress the two-row
mixed-only closures by raw graph features and instead compare the `536`
mixed-only states against small higher-support certificates. A useful target
is whether the `34` one-leaf-edge states have smaller three-row or four-row
unit supports that might expose a different structural mechanism.

## Session 2026-05-06, Theory Cycle 415

Timestamp: 2026-05-06 15:33 EEST.

### Subquestion

Do the `34` mixed-only states whose `G_mix` has exactly one edge admit exact
unit-weight three-row Kalmanson cancellations?

### Definitions and Assumptions

Use the same C19 selected-distance quotient and recorded fifth-pair frontier
as Theory Cycles 411--414. A **one-edge mixed-only state** is a recorded
fifth-pair state with:

- at least one mixed two-row meta-lemma trigger;
- no selected-selected R-R trigger; and
- exactly one edge in `G_mix`.

A **unit three-row support** is a set of three distinct forced Kalmanson rows,
each with coefficient `1`, whose quotient coefficient vectors sum exactly to
zero. Such a support gives an exact strict contradiction `0 > 0`.

### Attempted Proof Route

Replay the `18,270` recorded fifth-pair states, recover the `34` one-edge
mixed-only states from Theory Cycle 414, and then, for each of these states,
perform an exact pair-sum search over the `3300` forced Kalmanson rows to find
whether some three distinct rows have unit vector sum zero.

### Result

Proved finite recorded-frontier lemma:
**One-Edge Mixed-Only Three-Row Unit Closure**.

All `34` one-edge mixed-only states admit an exact unit-weight three-row
Kalmanson cancellation.

The replay re-derived the global bucket split:

```text
both:       17687
mixed only:   536
R-R only:      36
none:          11
```

The exact target label digest is:

```text
782988715bd386d2bcc29d6adc3c7661685c40fccb3e538d1c597ac70e3ff35d
```

The first-support digest for the deterministic search is:

```text
87f6afd4ed527c892a9d5a14eccda10f6efb29800aedaaddb54b94fc18ae6298
```

The support accounting is:

```text
one-edge mixed-only targets: 34
three-row unit supports found: 34
three-row unit supports missing: 0
distinct first supports: 15
```

The row-kind split among the deterministic first supports is:

```text
K1+K1+K2: 12
K1+K2+K2: 14
K2+K2+K2:  8
```

The leading literal supports are:

```text
8 states:
  K2(0,1,9,4)
  K1(0,11,4,2)
  K1(9,4,18,2)

3 states:
  K2(0,1,12,8)
  K1(0,12,10,2)
  K2(1,17,12,8)

3 states:
  K2(0,1,9,5)
  K2(1,12,9,5)
  K2(12,8,17,5)

3 states:
  K1(0,5,9,2)
  K2(0,14,5,9)
  K2(16,9,6,2)
```

The first recorded target examples are:

```text
c19_window_fifth_child_0156_0063_0053:
  K2(0,1,12,8)
  K1(0,12,10,2)
  K2(1,17,12,8)

c19_window_fifth_child_0199_0065_0036:
  K2(0,1,9,4)
  K1(0,11,4,2)
  K1(9,4,18,2)

c19_window_fifth_child_0256_0059_0027:
  K2(0,1,7,4)
  K1(0,7,4,15)
  K2(1,10,7,15)
```

### Limitations

- This is a finite existence result for the `34` one-edge mixed-only states
  only.
- The search stops at the first deterministic three-row support per state, so
  it does not classify all possible three-row supports.
- It does not prove that every mixed-only state has a three-row unit support;
  the other `502` mixed-only states remain outside this cycle.
- It does not address the `11` non-meta states or larger supports.
- It does not prove Erdos Problem #97 or produce a counterexample.

### Effect on the Attack

This is the first positive evidence that a higher-support mechanism may
organize a hard-looking part of the mixed-only forest residue. The one-edge
states were the most rigid leaf cases, and all of them have a three-row unit
closure independent of using the two-row leaf edge directly.

The next proof-facing lead is to increase the leaf-edge threshold: test
whether the `38` two-edge mixed-only states also all admit exact three-row unit
supports, and whether the same literal support families recur.

## Session 2026-05-06, Theory Cycle 455

Timestamp: 2026-05-06 19:23 EEST.

### Subquestion

Does the eight-template terminal-gate atlas from Theory Cycle 454 extend to a
meaningful entry theorem over the full recorded `288..479` fifth-pair
frontier, or is it genuinely terminal-gate-specific?

### Definitions and Assumptions

Use all fifth-pair children below the recorded fourth-pair survivors in

```text
data/certificates/c19_kalmanson_prefix_window_catalog_prefilter_sweep_288_479.json
```

This is the `10,350` fifth-child population already checked by the compact
catalog-prefilter sweep. A child is **atlas-covered** if it forces some cyclic
translation of one of the eight templates from Theory Cycle 454. A child is
**two-row closed** if the exact deterministic one/two-row Kalmanson lookup
finds a zero row or an opposite row pair.

### Attempted Proof Route

Replay every fifth-pair child in the recorded `288..479` sweep, checking the
per-window label digests against the source artifact. For each child:

1. build the forced Kalmanson row set;
2. run the deterministic one/two-row lookup;
3. test all translations of all eight terminal-gate atlas templates; and
4. record whether uncovered children are hard, meaning not two-row closed.

### Result

Counterexample to broad atlas entry:
**Full-Frontier Terminal-Atlas Noncoverage Obstruction**.

The replay reproduced the full fifth-pair frontier:

```text
fifth-pair children checked: 10350
fifth-pair label digest:
  62596c766c119b935f7894ea33a9b10c38a834fc3d5e250873c3f9f1fccc839f
```

The terminal-gate atlas covers only a minority of the full recorded frontier:

```text
atlas-covered children:      2385
atlas-uncovered children:    7965
atlas-uncovered digest:
  61b45df31c355ebb24ea5641e40f4df10023741c547329a83f388f4a27604685
```

However, every uncovered child is still two-row closed:

```text
atlas-uncovered with deterministic two-row certificate: 7965
atlas-uncovered without deterministic two-row certificate: 0
```

The hit-count histogram is:

```text
hit by 0 templates: 7965
hit by 1 template: 2184
hit by 2 templates:  187
hit by 3 templates:   14
```

The per-window uncovered counts are:

```text
320-351:  448 of  540
352-383:  579 of  720
384-415:  986 of 1350
416-447: 2886 of 4230
448-479: 3066 of 3510
```

Template coverage counts over all `10,350` fifth children are:

```text
T0_U0_catalog:              373
T1_U1_companion:            216
R0_U002_pair:               719
R1_U002_pair:               100
R2_U002_pair:               114
R3_U002_pair:               103
S4A_pair:                   916
C2_catalog_unit_support_002: 59
```

### Named Obstruction

**Terminal-Gate Specificity Obstruction.**

The eight-template atlas is not a broad entry theorem for the recorded
fifth-pair frontier. It exactly organizes the terminal-gate residue, but most
ordinary fifth children do not force any atlas template. The failure is not a
new hard obstruction, because every uncovered child is already closed by the
ordinary exact two-row lookup.

### Effect on the Attack

The correct proof-facing use of the atlas is now sharper:

```text
hard/no-two-row recorded child
  => terminal-gate atlas
```

remains consistent with the recorded data, but the stronger implication

```text
arbitrary recorded fifth child
  => terminal-gate atlas
```

is false. A future entry theorem should therefore be stated only for
children that evade the ordinary two-row cancellation lookup, or should be
augmented by a separate broad two-row atlas for the ordinary children.

### Limitations

- This is finite evidence for the recorded `288..479` fifth-pair frontier
  only.
- It tests only the eight terminal-gate templates from Theory Cycle 454.
- It does not classify the `7,965` uncovered two-row children by their own
  support-template families.
- It does not prove the hard-child entry implication outside this recorded
  frontier.
- It does not prove an all-order `C19_skew` obstruction.
- It does not prove Erdos Problem #97 and does not produce a counterexample.

  `codex/goal-erdos97-proof-or-counterexample`.
- `origin` is connected to `https://github.com/davidiach/erdos97.git`.
- The worktree was already dirty before this cycle; this cycle changes only
  `reports/codex_goal_erdos97_log.md`.
- No commit, push, or pull request was made.

### Validation

- One-off exact eight-template replay over all `10,350` reconstructed
  fifth-pair children: passed in 122.3 seconds, with source per-window label
  digests checked.
- The replay found zero atlas-uncovered children lacking a deterministic
  two-row certificate.

- `.venv/bin/python scripts/check_status_consistency.py`: passed.
- `git diff --check`: passed.

The overarching proof/counterexample goal remains open. No general proof and
no exact counterexample are claimed.

### Subquestion

After the support-size-2 and support-size-4 atlas reductions, is the entire
recorded `330`-occurrence terminal-gate population covered by a compact
translated zero-sum template atlas?

### Definitions and Assumptions

Use the recorded C19 `288..479` terminal-gate population from Theory Cycles
436--453. A template covers a terminal-gate occurrence if some cyclic
translation of all labels is forced by the occurrence's boundary state and
the translated rows have exact zero coefficient sum after quotienting by the
selected-distance equalities.

The tested atlas consists of:

```text
T0_U0_catalog:
  K1(0,3,10,11)
  K2(1,10,15,11)
  K2(3,15,12,6)

  K1(0,8,14,5)
  K1(18,15,4,1)
  K2(15,14,4,5)

  K1(0,3,14,4)
  K2(3,7,12,4)

  K1(5,0,13,3)

  K2(0,14,4,11)

  K2(9,0,6,14)

  K2(0,1,10,8)
  K1(0,14,8,11)
  K1(1,7,12,2)
  K2(1,3,7,8)

```

The catalog three-row support

```text
K1(0,16,5,2)
K1(1,9,15,6)
K2(16,15,5,6)
```

was also checked, but it has the same recorded coverage as
`T1_U1_companion` in this terminal-gate population and is therefore not needed
in the reduced atlas.

### Attempted Proof Route

Replay all terminal-gate occurrences from the compact catalog-prefilter sweep
artifact. For each occurrence:

1. classify its deterministic outcome as support-size-2 two-row,
   support-size-4 two-row, or catalog/non-two-row;
2. verify exact zero-sum for every listed template;
3. test all cyclic translations of every template against the forced rows of
   the occurrence; and
4. record uncovered occurrences and hit-count multiplicities.

### Result

Finite recorded-frontier synthesis:
**Recorded Terminal-Gate Eight-Template Atlas**.

The replay reproduced the full terminal-gate population:

```text

terminal-gate digest:
  b27a6cab5b574bcd9231f529fcceb4cf05639adcfebc94244a7eac43a32ff05f

  U0_missing_11R_terminal:  88
  U0_missing_12R_terminal:  56
  U002_missing_7L_terminal: 18
```

The outcome split is:

```text
support-size-2 two-row occurrences: 304
support-size-4 two-row occurrences: 18
catalog/non-two-row occurrences: 8

catalog/non-two-row digest:
  2dc4965b208144017b7aba73dba920c8c8f8f1eea93a2f84650eb4f5f484f0c8
support-size-2 digest:
  426e6bf5b3e6e5b154d7306aad8d31a28f753e082714034adcabedeb8fb68f4c
support-size-4 digest:
  53dba1a8323485481b5f7fdb67ad6d2f54bd1858d02b7e8571c301193cc6176e
```

The reduced eight-template atlas covers all `330` occurrences:

```text
uncovered occurrences: 0
uncovered digest:
  e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855

hit-count histogram:

  cc8e89c7dcf08ac40ed4ae3cfb65c63779009cf9cfc51b39d5d1277467254eb4
```

The individual template coverage counts are:

```text
T0_U0_catalog:             144
T1_U1_companion:           169
R0_U002_pair:               29
R1_U002_pair:               11
R2_U002_pair:               10
R3_U002_pair:               13
S4A_pair:                   20
C2_catalog_unit_support_002: 23
```

Every listed template was checked as an exact zero-sum vector in the
selected-distance quotient before the coverage replay.

### Named Reduction

**Terminal-Gate Atlas Reduction.**

Inside the recorded `288..479` terminal-gate frontier, every terminal-gate
occurrence is explained by one of eight translated zero-sum templates. This
replaces the earlier support-by-support tables with a compact proof-facing
object:

```text
U0-dominated behavior: T0_U0_catalog.
U1-dominated behavior: T1_U1_companion.
U002 residual behavior: R0-R3 plus C2.
support-size-4 residue: S4A plus T1.
```

### Effect on the Attack

The terminal-gate residue is no longer the main obstacle inside the recorded
window. The remaining proof gap has moved up one level:

```text
hard recorded fifth child
  => terminal-gate occurrence
```

or, more ambitiously,

```text
arbitrary C19 hard branch
  => a boundary state forcing one of the eight atlas templates.
```

This is the same Predicate-Entry Gap from Theory Cycle 433, sharpened by a
complete recorded terminal-gate atlas.

### Limitations

- This is finite evidence for the recorded `288..479` terminal-gate frontier
  only.
- The replay proves coverage of the `330` recorded terminal-gate occurrences,
  not all fifth-pair children and not all C19 cyclic orders.
- The eight-template atlas is not claimed minimal.
- The result does not prove that arbitrary hard branches enter the
  terminal-gate population.
- It does not prove an all-order `C19_skew` obstruction.
- It does not prove Erdos Problem #97 and does not produce a counterexample.

## Session 2026-05-06, Theory Cycle 453

Timestamp: 2026-05-06 19:06 EEST.

### Subquestion

Do the `18` recorded support-size-4 terminal-gate two-row occurrences admit a
compact translated unit-support atlas analogous to the support-size-2 atlas
from Theory Cycle 452?

### Definitions and Assumptions

Use the same recorded C19 `288..479` terminal-gate population and
selected-distance quotient as Theory Cycle 452. Restrict to terminal-gate
occurrences whose deterministic two-row certificate has quotient support-size
`4`, with coefficient profile `{-1,-1,+1,+1}`.

A support template is again identified up to cyclic translation modulo `19`.

### Attempted Proof Route

Replay all terminal-gate occurrences, isolate the deterministic support-size-4
subpopulation, and enumerate every forced unit-weight zero-sum support of size
at most `3` in each target state. Then quotient supports by translation,
reduce equal coverage masks, and solve the exact set-cover problem over the
resulting support schemas.

### Result

**Support-Size-4 Terminal-Gate Two-Template Atlas**.

The replay reproduced the support-type accounting:

```text

  53dba1a8323485481b5f7fdb67ad6d2f54bd1858d02b7e8571c301193cc6176e
```

All `18` support-size-4 occurrences lie in one translated four-class support
orbit:

```text

```

The gate split is:

```text
U1_missing_15R_terminal:  11
U002_missing_7L_terminal:  1
U0_missing_12R_terminal:   3
U0_missing_11R_terminal:   3
```

The exhaustive unit-support enumeration found:

```text
unit supports of size 1: 0
unit supports of size 2: 206
unit supports of size 3: 3026
translation-normalized support schemas: 1561
unique coverage masks: 67
nondominated coverage masks: 2
exact minimum cover size: 2

  4c0b06a353eddff1216065b537f4a4c4231d7008673dbe0f1e8dae4470084833
```

One exact minimum cover is:

```text
S4A_pair:
```


The exact quotient cancellations are:

```text
S4A_pair touches quotient classes 2, 5, 11, 22, each with total coefficient 0.
T1_U1_companion touches quotient classes 7, 11, 17, 26, 31, 60,
each with total coefficient 0.
```

### Named Reduction

**Single-Orbit Support-Size-4 Reduction.**

Unlike the support-size-2 side, the support-size-4 terminal-gate residue has
only one translated quotient-support orbit and needs only one new two-row
template beyond the previously recurring `T1_U1_companion`.

### Effect on the Attack

Combining Theory Cycles 452 and 453, the deterministic two-row side of the
recorded terminal-gate population now has a compact translated atlas:

```text
304 support-size-2 occurrences: covered by six templates.
 18 support-size-4 occurrences: covered by two templates, one already shared.
```

The only recorded terminal-gate occurrences not covered by this deterministic
two-row atlas are now the `8` catalog/non-two-row occurrences already studied
through catalog unit supports.

### Limitations

- This is finite evidence for the recorded `288..479` terminal-gate frontier
  only.
- It covers only the `18` deterministic support-size-4 two-row occurrences.
- The minimum cover is exact only over unit-weight supports of size at most
  `3` in these `18` states.
- It does not prove that arbitrary C19 branches enter the terminal-gate
  population or force these templates.
- It does not prove an all-order `C19_skew` obstruction.
- It does not prove Erdos Problem #97 and does not produce a counterexample.

## Session 2026-05-06, Theory Cycle 452

Timestamp: 2026-05-06 18:58 EEST.

### Subquestion

Do the two common three-row templates from Theory Cycles 450--451 cover all
recorded support-size-2 terminal-gate two-row children, or is there a residual
family requiring additional templates?

### Definitions and Assumptions

Use the same recorded C19 `288..479` terminal-gate population and the same
translated support-template language as Theory Cycles 450--451. Restrict to
terminal-gate occurrences whose deterministic two-row certificate has
support-size `2` in the selected-distance quotient. This is the `304`
occurrence population from Theory Cycle 443.

The two common templates are:

```text
T0_U0_catalog:
  K1(0,3,10,11)
  K2(1,10,15,11)
  K2(3,15,12,6)

T1_U1_companion:
  K1(0,8,14,5)
  K1(18,15,4,1)
  K2(15,14,4,5)
```

### Attempted Proof Route

Replay all `330` terminal-gate occurrences, recover the deterministic
support-size-2 subpopulation, and test whether a translated copy of either
`T0_U0_catalog` or `T1_U1_companion` is forced in each occurrence. For any
misses, enumerate all unit-weight zero-sum supports of size at most `3` in the
miss states, quotient by translation, and solve the exact residual set-cover
problem.

### Named Obstruction

**U002 Residual Four-Template Obstruction.**

The common two-template cover from Theory Cycles 450--451 is not universal
over support-size-2 terminal-gate children. Its exact failure set is small and
purely `U002`, but that residual does not collapse to one, two, or three
translated size-at-most-three unit templates; the exact residual minimum cover
has size `4`.

### Effect on the Attack

The support-size-2 terminal-gate side now has a compact recorded atlas:

```text
2 three-row templates for all U0/U1-dominated support-size-2 occurrences;
4 two-row templates for the U002 residual;
0 support-size-2 occurrences left uncovered.
```

This is a cleaner proof-facing object than the previous `14` exchange orbits
or the literal two-row tables. It suggests that the next exact subproblem
should leave exchange orbits behind and handle the remaining terminal-gate
population by support type:

```text
18 support-size-4 two-row occurrences;
8 catalog/non-two-row occurrences.
```

### Limitations

- This is finite evidence for the recorded `288..479` terminal-gate frontier
  only.
- It covers only the `304` support-size-2 deterministic two-row occurrences.
- The residual minimum cover is exact only over the `15` misses and over
  unit-weight supports of size at most `3`.
- The six-template atlas is not proved minimal over the full `304`
  occurrence population.
- It does not cover the `18` support-size-4 two-row occurrences or the `8`
  catalog/non-two-row occurrences.
- It does not prove an all-order `C19_skew` obstruction.
- It does not prove Erdos Problem #97 and does not produce a counterexample.

## Session 2026-05-06, Theory Cycle 451

### Definitions and Assumptions

Use the same fixed `C19_skew` selected-distance quotient, the same terminal
gate population, and the same support-size-at-most-three unit-support
language as Theory Cycle 450. The target exchange orbit is now the largest
observed support-size-2 orbit, represented by `(2,46)`.

Occurrences are counted as in Theory Cycle 443: the single overlapping

occurrence, so the target has `52` occurrences but `51` unique labels.

   every target occurrence;
3. quotient supports by cyclic translation;
4. reduce equal coverage masks; and
5. solve the exact set-cover problem over the resulting support schemas.

```text
unit supports of size 3: 5637

nondominated coverage masks: 15
```


The two templates are disjoint on the recorded target and cover all `52`
occurrences. Thus the largest orbit's six two-row clauses from Theory Cycle

### Named Reduction

**Two-Largest-Orbits Common Three-Row Template Pair.**

Across the two largest support-size-2 exchange orbits `(2,46)` and `(2,49)`,
the same translated pair of three-row unit supports covers all `101` recorded
occurrences:

```text
T0_U0_catalog covers all U0 occurrences.
T1_U1_companion covers all U1 occurrences and the single U002 overlap.
```

This is still recorded-frontier-local, but it is a stronger reusable
mathematical object than either orbit's literal two-row cover.

### Effect on the Attack

The repeated two-template cover suggests a real structural feature of the
terminal-gate frontier: the exchange orbit may be less important than the
terminal gate once size-3 unit supports are allowed. The next proof-facing
question is whether all large support-size-2 exchange orbits, or perhaps all

templates or a small extension of the same template pair.

## Session 2026-05-06, Theory Cycle 450

Timestamp: 2026-05-06 18:35 EEST.

### Subquestion

Does the second-largest support-size-2 exchange orbit from Theory Cycles
448--449 become substantially simpler if available certificates are allowed to
use unit-weight three-row Kalmanson cancellations instead of only two-row
opposite-vector clauses?

### Definitions and Assumptions

Use the same fixed `C19_skew` selected-distance quotient with offsets
`[-8,-3,5,9]`, the same `330` terminal-gate occurrences, and the same
second-largest exchange orbit represented by `(2,49)`.

A **unit three-row support** is a set of three forced Kalmanson rows, each with
coefficient `1`, whose coefficient vectors sum to zero after quotienting by
the selected-distance equalities. Such a support gives the strict
contradiction `0 > 0` for every completion of the boundary state forcing the
three rows.

A support is considered up to cyclic translation of all labels modulo `19`,
using the verified translation symmetry of the selected-distance quotient.

### Attempted Proof Route

Replay the recorded terminal-gate population from

```text
data/certificates/c19_kalmanson_prefix_window_catalog_prefilter_sweep_288_479.json
```

Then:

1. reproduce the support-size-2 exchange orbit `(2,49)`;
2. for each of its `49` target occurrences, enumerate every forced
   unit-weight zero-sum support of size at most `3`;
3. quotient every support by cyclic translation;
4. reduce equal coverage masks; and
5. solve the exact set-cover problem over the resulting support schemas.

### Result

Proved finite recorded-orbit lemma:
**Second-Orbit Two-Template Three-Row Unit Cover Lemma**.

The replay first reproduced the earlier target exactly:

```text
terminal-gate occurrences: 330
terminal-gate digest:
  b27a6cab5b574bcd9231f529fcceb4cf05639adcfebc94244a7eac43a32ff05f

support-size-2 two-row children: 304
second exchange-orbit target count: 49
second exchange-orbit target digest:
  3f139076f9552f38a2ae49592beaa9d6a44b7a867df7522105226202238d1156
```

For those `49` targets, the size-at-most-three unit-support enumeration found:

```text
unit supports of size 1: 0
unit supports of size 2: 765
unit supports of size 3: 9908
translation-normalized support schemas: 3740
unique coverage masks: 190
nondominated coverage masks: 14
exact minimum cover size: 2
support-incidence mask digest:
  6519daf9dfbaf691e4babc02d96c9fd3af6c0149074e0cd8c9a9855ec5f415d2
```

One exact minimum cover is the following two translated three-row templates.

Template `T0_U0_catalog`:

```text
K1(0,3,10,11)
K2(1,10,15,11)
K2(3,15,12,6)
```

It covers `26` target occurrences, all in the two `U0` gates:

```text
U0_missing_11R_terminal: 19
U0_missing_12R_terminal:  7
```

Template `T1_U1_companion`:

```text
K1(0,8,14,5)
K1(18,15,4,1)
K2(15,14,4,5)
```

It covers `23` target occurrences, all in the `U1` gate:

```text
U1_missing_15R_terminal: 23
```

The two templates are disjoint on the recorded target and cover all `49`
occurrences:

```text
covered by neither template: 0
covered by T0 only:          26
covered by T1 only:          23
covered by both templates:    0

T0 label digest:
  b75d83ddde9b078608dddf7a56ebed3a78480b0e2096a6844ba73c666ddd03e7
T1 label digest:
  69434f0a40a5592ea552d5990e71e4049085ad165b89a26e93025f4e5888e901
template digest:
  03bb10f1643df8bdf7f796e2bfafff8e6bf2d7e468cd150eb2ea926b8b78e9be
```

The exact quotient cancellation for the two templates is:

```text
T0 touches quotient classes 2, 8, 23, 26, 43, 89, each with total coefficient 0.
T1 touches quotient classes 7, 11, 17, 26, 31, 60, each with total coefficient 0.
```

Thus the second exchange orbit, which resisted compression in the two-row
language, collapses cleanly after allowing unit three-row supports.

### Named Reduction

**Second-Orbit U0/U1 Three-Row Partition.**

On the recorded second-largest exchange orbit `(2,49)`, the support mechanism
is not intrinsically an eight-clause phenomenon. The eight two-row schemas
from Theory Cycles 448--449 are shadows of two three-row unit mechanisms:
one for the `U0` part and one for the `U1` part.

### Effect on the Attack

This is a useful proof-facing improvement. The second orbit now has a
human-scale structural description:

```text
U0 second-orbit target => translated T0_U0_catalog support
U1 second-orbit target => translated T1_U1_companion support
```

The first template is the already cataloged `unit_support_000`; the second is
a companion three-row unit support not isolated by the two-row cover audit.
This suggests revisiting the other large support-size-2 exchange orbits with
three-row unit templates before trying to force a large literal two-row cover.

### Limitations

- This is finite evidence for the recorded `288..479` terminal-gate frontier
  only.
- It covers only the second-largest support-size-2 exchange orbit `(2,49)`.
- The enumeration is exhaustive only for unit-weight supports of size at most
  `3` in the recorded target states.
- It does not prove that arbitrary C19 branches enter this orbit or force
  either template.
- It does not classify the other support-size-2 exchange orbits or the
  support-size-4 two-row children.
- It does not prove an all-order `C19_skew` obstruction.
- It does not prove Erdos Problem #97 and does not produce a counterexample.

## Session 2026-05-06, Theory Cycle 449

Timestamp: 2026-05-06 18:21 EEST.

### Subquestion

Does the eight-clause cover for the second-largest exchange orbit from Theory
Cycle 448 collapse after quotienting row-pair clauses by the verified
translation symmetry of the fixed `C19_skew` selected-distance quotient?

### Definitions and Assumptions

Use the same second-largest exchange orbit represented by `(2,49)`. A literal
two-row clause is normalized under cyclic translation by applying

```text
x -> x+t mod 19
```

to every label in both Kalmanson rows, for all `t=0,...,18`, and choosing a
canonical representative. This is the same translation group used in Theory
Cycle 443; no reflection or nontranslation affine symmetry is assumed.

A **translation-schema clause** covers a recorded target occurrence if at
least one literal available two-row clause in that schema is forced by that
occurrence.

### Attempted Proof Route

Replay the `49` target occurrences from Theory Cycle 448. For each occurrence,
enumerate all literal available exact two-row clauses, map each literal clause
to its translation schema, and solve the exact set-cover problem over the
resulting schema-to-record incidence table. Then compare the result with the
literal eight-clause cover from Theory Cycle 448.

### Result

Failed compression:
**Second-Orbit Translation-Schema Cover Obstruction**.

The exact replay found:

```text
target records: 49
literal available exact two-row clauses: 225
translation-schema clauses: 207
nondominated translation-schema clauses: 17
exact minimum translation-schema cover size: 8
```

Thus translation normalization does not reduce the second-orbit cover size.
The literal eight-clause cover from Theory Cycle 448 maps to eight distinct
translation schemas, and no seven translation schemas cover all `49` target
records.

One exact schema-level minimum cover is:

```text
K2(0,15,6,10) + K2(17,0,6,8)
  covers 9 records

K1(0,9,13,4) + K2(0,2,11,13)
  covers 3 records

K1(0,12,14,4) + K2(0,2,12,10)
  covers 20 records

K1(0,6,14,11) + K2(0,17,6,8)
  covers 4 records

K1(0,11,13,2) + K1(0,13,9,4)
  covers 2 records

K1(0,3,8,5) + K2(3,15,12,5)
  covers 8 records

K1(0,3,8,12) + K2(1,8,17,12)
  covers 12 records

K1(0,8,14,11) + K2(0,8,11,5)
  covers 10 records
```

The largest schema remains the same structural clause already noticed in
literal form:

```text
K1(0,12,14,4) + K2(0,2,12,10)
```

which is the translation normalization of

```text
K1(1,13,15,5) + K2(1,3,13,11).
```

It covers `20` of the `49` target records.

### Named Obstruction

**Translation-Schema Noncompression Obstruction.**

For the second-largest exchange orbit, the spread is not merely a failure of
literal labels. After quotienting by the full verified translation symmetry of
the selected-distance quotient, an eight-schema cover is still necessary in
the audited available-clause language.

### Effect on the Attack

This rules out the simplest symmetry explanation for the second orbit's
clause spread. The remaining compression options are:

1. use a more abstract row-pair schema than literal translated Kalmanson rows;
2. allow higher-support certificates; or
3. accept that this orbit contributes several genuinely distinct residual
   mechanisms.

The next exact subproblem should focus on the three low-coverage schema
families, especially the one- and two-record clauses, and test whether they
admit three-row unit closures or a common higher-support pattern.

### Limitations

- This is a finite schema-cover audit for the recorded second-largest exchange
  orbit only.
- The schema quotient uses translations only; this is intentional because the
  verified selected-distance quotient automorphism group is translation-only.
- The minimum cover is over available two-row clauses and their translation
  schemas, not higher-support or more semantic clauses.
- It does not classify the other `12` support-size-2 exchange orbits or the
  `18` support-size-4 two-row children.
- It does not prove an all-order `C19_skew` obstruction.
- It does not prove Erdos Problem #97 and does not produce a counterexample.

- Current branch during the cycle:
  `codex/goal-erdos97-proof-or-counterexample`.
- `origin` is connected to `https://github.com/davidiach/erdos97.git`.
- The worktree was already dirty before this cycle; this cycle changes only

## Session 2026-05-06, Theory Cycle 448

Timestamp: 2026-05-06 18:18 EEST.

### Subquestion

Does the second-largest support-size-2 exchange orbit from Theory Cycle 443,
represented by `(2,49)`, also admit a compact cover by available exact two-row
Kalmanson clauses?

### Definitions and Assumptions

Use the same fixed `C19_skew` quotient, terminal-gate population, and
row-forcing-clause language as Theory Cycle 447. Here, however, do not start
from a hand-picked candidate table. For every target occurrence, enumerate all
literal available two-row Kalmanson clauses: unordered pairs of forced
nonzero rows whose quotient coefficient vectors are exact negatives.

Then group clauses by their literal row pair, discard dominated clauses whose
covered record set is contained in another clause's record set, and solve the
remaining finite set-cover problem exactly.

### Attempted Proof Route

Replay the terminal-gate occurrences and restrict to deterministic
support-size-2 two-row children whose exchanged class pair lies in the
translation orbit represented by `(2,49)`. For each target record:

1. enumerate all available exact two-row cancellation clauses from its forced
   Kalmanson rows;
2. build a clause-to-record incidence table;
3. remove dominated literal clauses; and
4. brute-force the exact minimum cover over the nondominated clauses.

### Result

Finite recorded-orbit cover and obstruction:
**Second Exchange Orbit Eight-Clause Cover Lemma**.

The replay reproduced the second-largest orbit:

```text
gate occurrences: 49
unique fifth-child labels: 49
target label digest:
  3f139076f9552f38a2ae49592beaa9d6a44b7a867df7522105226202238d1156
target record digest:
  bb27cf0c06d05de5ca8aff4b6c90966ae23c9121af5b84b1aa35be1d31272ee8
```

The deterministic local lookup uses `10` support lines:

```text
15  K1(0,13,11,2) + K2(0,8,13,5)
 9  K1(0,13,11,2) + K2(0,4,13,9)
 7  K1(0,11,13,2) + K2(0,8,5,13)
 5  K1(0,11,13,2) + K2(0,4,9,13)
 3  K2(1,3,12,14) + K1(1,10,14,5)
 3  K2(1,3,9,11) + K2(3,18,9,13)
 3  K2(1,18,7,9) + K1(1,7,15,12)
 2  K1(0,11,13,2) + K1(0,13,9,4)
 1  K1(0,13,11,2) + K1(0,9,13,4)
 1  K2(1,18,7,9) + K1(1,7,11,16)
```

The exhaustive available-clause audit found:

```text
available literal exact two-row clauses: 225
nondominated clauses after cover reduction: 14
exact minimum cover size: 8
```

One exact minimum cover is:

```text
K2(1,18,9,11) + K2(3,18,11,13)
  covers 9 records

K1(1,10,14,5) + K2(1,3,12,14)
  covers 3 records

K1(1,13,15,5) + K2(1,3,13,11)
  covers 20 records

K1(1,7,15,12) + K2(1,18,7,9)
  covers 4 records

K1(0,3,8,12) + K2(1,8,17,12)
  covers 12 records

K1(0,13,11,2) + K1(0,9,13,4)
  covers 1 record

K1(0,11,13,2) + K1(0,13,9,4)
  covers 2 records

K1(0,10,4,15) + K1(0,4,8,13)
  covers 5 records
```

The greedy cover found the same size, and the exact search over the `14`
nondominated clauses found no cover of size at most `7`.

### Named Obstruction

**Second-Orbit Clause-Cover Spread Obstruction.**

Unlike the largest exchange orbit, which collapsed from eight deterministic
support lines to a six-clause cover, the second-largest orbit still needs
eight clauses even after enumerating all available literal exact two-row
clauses and removing dominated clauses. Thus this orbit does not currently
support a very small one- or two-mechanism proof.

### Effect on the Attack

The available-clause method still compresses the deterministic support list:

```text
10 deterministic support lines
8 exact available row-forcing clauses
```

but the gain is modest. The repeated high-coverage clause

```text
K1(1,13,15,5) + K2(1,3,13,11)
```

covers `20` records and also appeared in the largest-orbit work, so it is a
promising reusable clause. The singleton and two-record clauses are the main
obstruction to a compact proof table for this orbit.

The next exact subproblem is to analyze whether those low-coverage clauses are
forced by rare boundary features that can be merged with nearby high-coverage
clauses, or whether they represent genuinely separate residual mechanisms.

### Limitations

- This is a finite cover for the recorded second-largest exchange orbit only.
- The exact minimum cover is over literal available two-row clauses in the
  recorded target records; it does not include higher-support certificates or
  more abstract schematic clauses.
- It does not prove that arbitrary C19 branches enter any clause in the cover.
- It does not classify the other `12` support-size-2 exchange orbits or the
  `18` support-size-4 two-row children.
- It does not prove an all-order `C19_skew` obstruction.
- It does not prove Erdos Problem #97 and does not produce a counterexample.

## Session 2026-05-06, Theory Cycle 447

Timestamp: 2026-05-06 18:16 EEST.

### Subquestion

Can the entire largest support-size-2 exchange orbit from Theory Cycle 443 be
covered by a small table of human-readable row-forcing clauses, after allowing
available non-deterministic row pairs from Theory Cycles 445--446?

### Definitions and Assumptions

Use the fixed `C19_skew` selected-distance quotient, the `330` terminal-gate
occurrences, and the target exchange orbit represented by `(2,46)`.

A **row-forcing clause** is a pair of forced Kalmanson rows whose quotient
coefficient vectors cancel exactly. The clause covers a terminal-gate
occurrence if both rows are forced by that occurrence's boundary state. The
clause need not be the deterministic support returned by the local two-row
lookup.

### Attempted Proof Route

Start with the eight deterministic support lines seen in the largest exchange
orbit, then add the two available non-deterministic improvements from Theory
Cycles 445--446:

- use `C_U1_13_11` instead of the `U1 free 12 left4` deterministic row pair
  when it is available; and
- use the uniform `A_17_middle8` pair for the `U0 free 17` target bucket.

Then audit every target occurrence, compute exact quotient cancellation for
each candidate clause, and brute-force the minimum set cover among the eight
candidate clauses.

### Result

Proved finite recorded-orbit cover:
**Largest Exchange Orbit Six-Clause Cover Lemma**.

The exact replay reproduced the orbit:

```text
gate occurrences: 52
unique fifth-child labels: 51
target label digest:
  4351afa10f122aeb824a06759c7c9a467ae986202f10752d4fb3d23a4e599611
clause record digest:
  cb3d18c90afb980a90ec7def6fe298bba877d5558a86f0262b906a1a8b3ce170
missing records under the candidate clause table: 0
```

Among the eight candidate clauses, the exact minimum cover is unique and has
size `6`:

```text
A_17_middle8
B_4_14_common10
C_U1_13_11
E_U1_free12_left17
G_U1_free8
H_U1_free18
```

The six clauses are:

```text
A_17_middle8:
  K1(0,10,12,2) + K2(0,17,8,12)
  covers 21 gate occurrences

B_4_14_common10:
  K1(0,10,12,2) + K2(0,4,14,12)
  covers 11 gate occurrences

C_U1_13_11:
  K2(1,3,13,11) + K1(1,13,15,5)
  covers 17 gate occurrences

E_U1_free12_left17:
  K1(0,12,10,2) + K2(0,17,12,8)
  covers 3 gate occurrences

G_U1_free8:
  K2(1,3,8,12) + K1(1,8,10,18)
  covers 1 gate occurrence

H_U1_free18:
  K2(1,3,12,8) + K2(1,18,8,10)
  covers 1 gate occurrence
```

The coverage counts are not disjoint; the candidate table has seven
two-clause overlaps. The hit-count histogram over all target occurrences is:

```text
covered by 1 candidate clause: 45
covered by 2 candidate clauses:  7
```

The two dropped deterministic clauses have no unique record after allowing
the available alternatives:

```text
D_U1_free12_left4:
  K1(0,12,10,2) + K2(0,4,12,14)
  unique records after all candidates: 0

F_7_11_17_9:
  K1(0,7,11,2) + K2(0,17,7,9)
  unique records after all candidates: 0
```

Every listed clause was checked as an exact zero coefficient sum in the
selected-distance quotient.

### Effect on the Attack

This is the first compact table for a full exchange-orbit family from Theory
Cycle 443. It reduces the largest support-size-2 orbit from:

```text
52 gate occurrences
8 deterministic support lines
```

to:

```text
6 row-forcing clauses
```

The table is still finite and recorded-frontier-local, but it gives a
proof-facing object: six explicit row-pair lemmas whose boundary forcing can
be checked by hand. This suggests the next exact subproblem: repeat the same
available-clause cover audit for the second-largest exchange orbit, and see
whether a small number of row-forcing clauses again covers the orbit.

### Limitations

- This is a finite cover of the recorded largest exchange orbit only.
- It uses candidate row-pair clauses found by replaying the recorded frontier;
  it does not prove that arbitrary C19 branches must enter one of these six
  clauses.
- The minimum cover is with respect to the eight audited candidate clauses,
  not all possible row-pair clauses.
- It does not classify the other `13` support-size-2 exchange orbits or the
  `18` support-size-4 two-row children.
- It does not prove an all-order `C19_skew` obstruction.
- It does not prove Erdos Problem #97 and does not produce a counterexample.
